heroOverview: Gastroparesis is a chronic disorder in which the stomach empties far more slowly than normal despite the absence of a mechanical blockage. The stomach's muscular contractions are too weak or poorly coordinated to move food into the small intestine at a normal pace, so food lingers for hours or days. Diabetes accounts for roughly 30% of cases because sustained high blood glucose damages the vagus nerve that drives gastric motility. An estimated 1.8% of the U.S. adult population carries a gastroparesis diagnosis, and the true burden is likely higher because mild cases are often misattributed to functional dyspepsia. Symptoms range from persistent nausea and early satiety to dangerous blood-glucose swings in diabetic patients. Treatment rarely cures the condition but can control symptoms well enough for most people to maintain adequate nutrition and quality of life.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1.8% of U.S. adults report a physician diagnosis; age-adjusted prevalence estimated at 24.2 per 100,000 in women and 9.6 per 100,000 in men (Choung et al., Gut 2012)
keyStats.demographics: Women affected roughly three times more often than men; diabetic patients have a 10-year cumulative incidence of approximately 5% (type 1) and 1% (type 2)
keyStats.avgAge: Most commonly diagnosed between ages 20 and 60; diabetic gastroparesis peaks in middle age
keyStats.globalCases: Prevalence data outside North America are limited; the condition is likely underdiagnosed worldwide due to limited access to gastric-emptying scintigraphy

definition: Gastroparesis (ICD-10: K31.84) is a syndrome of objectively delayed gastric emptying in the absence of mechanical obstruction, causing symptoms including nausea, vomiting, early satiety, bloating, and abdominal pain. The hallmark finding on four-hour gastric emptying scintigraphy is retention of more than 10% of a standardised solid meal at four hours. The pathophysiology centres on loss or dysfunction of the interstitial cells of Cajal (the stomach's pacemaker cells), injury to the enteric nervous system, or damage to the extrinsic vagal innervation. Diabetic gastroparesis — the best-studied subtype — arises from autonomic neuropathy compounded by hyperglycaemia-induced impairment of nitric-oxide-mediated smooth-muscle relaxation. Idiopathic gastroparesis, the most common type, often follows an acute viral illness and may represent post-infectious autonomic damage. Post-surgical gastroparesis most often follows vagotomy, fundoplication, or Whipple resection. Because gastric emptying rate is highly variable from day to day, and because symptoms correlate only modestly with emptying time, diagnosis requires standardised testing and exclusion of mimics including peptic ulcer disease, gastric outlet obstruction, and functional dyspepsia. The specialty responsible for its evaluation and management is gastroenterology, occasionally in collaboration with endocrinology for diabetic cases.

typesClassification.description: Occurs in 5-12% of patients with long-standing type 1 diabetes and 1-2% of those with type 2. Hyperglycaemia worsens motility acutely, and chronic hyperglycaemia destroys vagal fibres and interstitial cells of Cajal over time, creating a vicious cycle.

The most common type, accounting for 35-50% of cases. It often develops after a flu-like illness, suggesting viral injury to gastric pacemaker cells or the vagus nerve; symptoms sometimes improve over months to years in younger patients.
Results from inadvertent vagal injury during fundoplication, Whipple procedure, oesophagectomy, or gastrectomy. Often presents within days of surgery but can persist or emerge weeks later.
Narcotics (opioids), GLP-1 receptor agonists, anticholinergics, and tricyclic antidepressants are the most common drug causes; the disorder typically reverses when the offending agent is stopped.

Parkinson disease, scleroderma, hypothyroidism, and amyloidosis can each damage gastric neuromuscular function through distinct mechanisms. Identifying and treating the underlying disease is the primary management goal.

primarySymptoms: Nausea that is present on most days, often worst in the morning or after meals, and persists even when the stomach appears empty — the single most common symptom, reported by over 90% of patients.
Vomiting of partially digested food, sometimes hours after eating or overnight, reflecting the prolonged retention of a meal that should have emptied into the small intestine.

Early satiety — feeling uncomfortably full after only a few bites or a small portion — caused by impaired fundic relaxation and slow pyloric emptying that leave the stomach distended even on a small meal.
Post-meal bloating and abdominal distension, often lasting three or more hours after eating, accompanied by audible gurgling or visible bloating above the navel.
Upper abdominal pain or discomfort, ranging from a dull ache to intermittent cramping, which worsens after large or high-fat meals and may radiate to the mid-back.

Unintentional weight loss resulting from reduced food intake driven by persistent nausea and fear of post-meal discomfort; loss of more than 5 kg suggests a nutritional problem requiring intervention.

Unpredictable blood-glucose levels in diabetic patients — the slow, variable delivery of nutrients to the small intestine makes post-prandial glucose control erratic despite consistent insulin dosing.
Heartburn and regurgitation from retained gastric contents refluxing back into the oesophagus, sometimes misdiagnosed initially as GERD.
Nutritional deficiencies including protein-energy malnutrition, vitamin B12 depletion, and iron deficiency in patients whose intake stays severely restricted for months.
Fatigue and reduced capacity for daily activity stemming from poor caloric intake, disrupted sleep from overnight nausea, and the psychological burden of a chronic unpredictable illness.
earlyWarningSigns: Feeling uncomfortably full after eating less than a third of a normal-sized meal.
Food regurgitating several hours after it was eaten, or finding undigested food in vomit.
Nausea that appears without an obvious trigger and lingers throughout the morning.
A pattern of blood-glucose readings rising two to four hours after a meal rather than at the expected 60-90 minute peak.
emergencySigns: Vomiting that is so frequent or severe that fluids and medications cannot be kept down, causing dehydration with dry mouth, reduced urination, and dizziness on standing.
Prolonged blood glucose above 16 mmol/L (290 mg/dL) or below 3 mmol/L (54 mg/dL) that cannot be corrected at home, signalling that gastroparesis is preventing predictable nutrient delivery.
Sudden worsening of abdominal pain beyond the usual pattern, which could indicate a bezoar causing obstruction or another acute complication.
Inability to eat or drink for more than 48 hours, producing severe weakness, weight loss, or confusion.

causes.description: Sustained hyperglycaemia damages the vagus nerve and the myenteric plexus, reducing the frequency and force of antral contractions and impairing pyloric relaxation. The nitric-oxide-producing inhibitory neurons are particularly vulnerable, so the pylorus stays tonically closed rather than opening to let food through.

An acute viral illness — most often a non-specific upper respiratory infection or gastroenteritis — can trigger inflammation that damages the vagal branches supplying the stomach and depletes interstitial cells of Cajal, the pacemaker cells that generate the stomach's slow-wave electrical rhythm. This is thought to account for the majority of idiopathic cases.

Procedures near the gastro-oesophageal junction or pancreas can sever or stretch vagal trunks that coordinate gastric motility. Even incomplete vagal injury can reduce antral pump function by 50% or more, explaining why delayed emptying persists after the surgical site has healed.

Opioid receptors are densely expressed in the enteric nervous system; any opioid analgesic reduces antral contractility, increases pyloric tone, and suppresses the migrating motor complex, the interdigestive 'housekeeping' wave that clears residue. GLP-1 receptor agonists and anticholinergics act through distinct pathways with similar end results.

Parkinson disease involves loss of dopaminergic neurons in the gut wall; scleroderma replaces smooth muscle with collagen, removing contractile tissue; hypothyroidism slows motor nerve conduction. Each produces delayed emptying through a different but well-characterised mechanism.

Even in a person without established neuropathy, blood glucose above approximately 10 mmol/L (180 mg/dL) acutely impairs antral contraction amplitude by around 30% and reduces the gastric electrical slow-wave frequency, worsening symptoms and emptying times in anyone with pre-existing gastroparesis.

riskFactors.description: Cumulative autonomic nerve damage from years of glycaemic exposure is the strongest modifiable mediator, though diabetes itself is a non-modifiable diagnosis. Risk rises sharply after 10 years of poor control.
HbA1c above 9% is associated with a significantly higher prevalence of delayed gastric emptying; tightening control can partially restore motility in patients without complete vagal destruction.

Women are diagnosed roughly three times more often than men; hormonal influences on gastric motility (progesterone slows emptying; oestrogen has complex effects) are thought to partly explain the sex difference.

riskFactors.factor: Prior upper gastrointestinal or pancreatic surgery
riskFactors.description: Any operation near the oesophagogastric junction or pancreatic head carries a risk of vagal injury; fundoplication carries a 3-10% incidence of post-operative dysmotility.

Chronic opioid therapy suppresses gastric motility through mu-receptor activation throughout the enteric nervous system; risk is dose-dependent and largely reversible with opioid rotation or cessation.
Thyroid hormone deficiency slows enteric nerve conduction; treating the hypothyroidism normalises gastric emptying in most affected patients.
Post-infectious gastroparesis typically affects younger patients without other risk factors; prognosis is better than in diabetic cases, with gradual improvement in around 80% within 12-24 months.

riskFactors.factor: Eating disorders and severely restricted diets
riskFactors.description: Prolonged caloric restriction or anorexia nervosa disrupt the migrating motor complex and can produce functional delayed emptying that may become chronic.

affectedDemographics: Women aged 20-60 years, who bear the largest disease burden
People with type 1 diabetes of more than 10 years' duration
People with type 2 diabetes and poor long-term glycaemic control
Patients who have undergone fundoplication, oesophagectomy, or Whipple procedure
Younger adults following a viral gastroenteritis or respiratory illness
Patients with Parkinson disease or systemic sclerosis
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing gastroparesis requires demonstrating objectively delayed gastric emptying after mechanical obstruction has been excluded. The standard test is four-hour solid-phase gastric emptying scintigraphy using a 99mTc-sulphur-colloid egg meal prepared according to the American Neurogastroenterology and Motility Society consensus protocol; retention above 10% at four hours or above 60% at two hours defines delayed emptying. Before ordering scintigraphy, an upper endoscopy is almost always performed to rule out pyloric stenosis, peptic ulcer, or malignancy. Barium or CT imaging may be used when obstruction is suspected. Wireless motility capsule testing offers an alternative when scintigraphy is unavailable and provides transit data for the entire GI tract. Because acute hyperglycaemia slows emptying, the scintigraphy should be performed when blood glucose is between 4 and 15 mmol/L (70–270 mg/dL) and opioids must be withheld for 48 hours to avoid a false-positive result. Gastric antral manometry and electrogastrography are available at specialist centres for cases where the diagnosis remains uncertain or for guiding placement of a gastric stimulator. Functional dyspepsia, cyclic vomiting syndrome, and rumination syndrome are the main clinical mimics and are distinguished by symptom pattern, absence of delayed emptying on testing, and, in rumination, the characteristic effortless regurgitation of undigested food within minutes of eating.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The reference standard: quantifies what proportion of a standardised radiolabelled meal remains in the stomach at one, two, and four hours.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You eat a small egg-white meal labelled with a tiny amount of radiotracer, then stand in front of a gamma camera for brief scans at 0, 1, 2, and 4 hours. There is no injection and no sedation. The four-hour test is an outpatient procedure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes mechanical causes — pyloric stenosis, peptic ulcer, gastric cancer, and bezoar — before attributing delayed emptying to a motility disorder.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin flexible camera is passed through the mouth to examine the oesophagus, stomach, and duodenum under sedation; the procedure takes 15-20 minutes and you need someone to drive you home.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures gastric emptying time along with small-bowel and colon transit by tracking pressure, pH, and temperature as an ingestible sensor travels through the GI tract.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You swallow a capsule the size of a large vitamin; a recorder worn on the wrist captures data for three to five days while you follow a standard diet. The capsule passes naturally.

diagnosticTests.test: CT abdomen with oral and IV contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out partial mechanical obstruction, gastric outlet lesion, or extrinsic compression before scintigraphy is arranged.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A CT scan takes five to ten minutes; you drink contrast beforehand, and an IV contrast injection is given during scanning. Results are typically available within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly measures the pressure waves generated by antral contractions, distinguishing neuropathic from myopathic causes and confirming pylorospasm.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin pressure-sensing tube is placed through the nose or mouth into the stomach, usually under light sedation; you then eat a test meal while pressure patterns are recorded for about two hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood tests including HbA1c, TSH, anti-nuclear antibody, and metabolic panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies or excludes secondary causes including uncontrolled diabetes, hypothyroidism, and connective-tissue disease, and assesses nutritional status.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw from the arm; results available within 24-48 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of gastroparesis is stepped, with dietary modification and glycaemic optimisation forming the foundation. Patients are advised to eat small, frequent low-fat and low-fibre meals that empty more easily through a poorly functioning pylorus; in some cases daily caloric intake must be redistributed across five or six small meals. Liquid-predominant or blended food is often better tolerated than solid meals. When dietary changes alone are insufficient, prokinetic medications are added to enhance gastric contractility; metoclopramide is the only FDA-approved prokinetic for gastroparesis and is used at the lowest effective dose for the shortest necessary duration because of the risk of tardive dyskinesia with prolonged exposure. Domperidone is not FDA-approved in the USA but is used in Europe, Canada, and Australia and has a lower risk of central nervous system side effects. Erythromycin acts as a motilin agonist and improves emptying but loses efficacy within weeks due to receptor downregulation. Antiemetic agents including ondansetron, promethazine, and prochlorperazine address nausea and vomiting without directly improving emptying. For patients with intractable symptoms, gastric electrical stimulation with the Enterra device provides meaningful relief of nausea and vomiting in about 50-60% of those who fail medical therapy, though it has limited effect on emptying itself. Endoscopic or surgical pyloromyotomy (per-oral endoscopic myotomy, POEM, applied to the pylorus — G-POEM) is an emerging procedure that reduces pyloric resistance and has shown promising early results. Patients who cannot maintain adequate oral nutrition may require temporary nasojejunal feeding, and a small minority with severe refractory disease need long-term jejunostomy tube feeding or, rarely, total parenteral nutrition.

medicalTreatments.name: Metoclopramide
medicalTreatments.description: A dopamine D2 receptor antagonist that enhances antral contractions and accelerates gastric emptying; it is the only FDA-approved prokinetic for gastroparesis. Use is limited to 12 weeks by the black-box warning for tardive dyskinesia, though short courses are generally safe at 5-10 mg before meals.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces nausea and vomiting in approximately 40-60% of patients in short-term trials; accelerates emptying by roughly 30% compared with placebo.

medicalTreatments.name: Domperidone
medicalTreatments.description: A peripherally acting dopamine antagonist that does not cross the blood-brain barrier as readily as metoclopramide, offering prokinetic effects with lower rates of extrapyramidal side effects. Available in Europe, Canada, and Australia; obtainable in the USA through the FDA Expanded Access programme.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical improvement in nausea and vomiting reported in 60-70% of patients in open-label and controlled European studies; modestly accelerates gastric emptying.

medicalTreatments.name: Erythromycin (low-dose motilin agonist)
medicalTreatments.description: At sub-antibiotic doses (40-125 mg three times daily oral or 3 mg/kg IV), erythromycin binds motilin receptors on the antral muscle and strongly accelerates gastric emptying. Intravenous erythromycin is particularly useful for short-term hospitalised patients who cannot tolerate oral intake.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IV erythromycin reduces gastric emptying time by approximately 50% acutely; oral benefit diminishes after four to eight weeks due to motilin-receptor downregulation.

medicalTreatments.name: Ondansetron (antiemetic)
medicalTreatments.description: A 5-HT3 serotonin receptor antagonist that controls nausea and vomiting without prokinetic effects; it improves symptom burden and quality of life in patients whose primary complaint is nausea rather than pain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces nausea severity scores by 30-50% in clinical practice; does not improve gastric emptying rates.

medicalTreatments.name: Tricyclic antidepressants (low-dose, e.g. amitriptyline 10-25 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: At doses far below antidepressant levels, TCAs modulate visceral pain, reduce nausea via central antihistamine and anti-muscarinic effects, and improve quality of life. They can worsen emptying at higher doses and are generally avoided when gastroparesis is predominantly a motility problem.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduced nausea scores and improved global symptoms in 40-50% of idiopathic gastroparesis patients in one randomised trial, comparable to buspirone.

medicalTreatments.name: Glycaemic optimisation in diabetic patients
medicalTreatments.description: Continuous glucose monitoring with insulin pump therapy or closed-loop systems that prevent acute hyperglycaemia above 10 mmol/L can reduce gastroparesis severity significantly; each 1% HbA1c reduction is associated with measurably faster emptying.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Tighter glycaemic control can improve gastric emptying by 20-40% in patients without complete vagal loss; benefit is greatest when HbA1c is lowered from above 9% to below 7%.

surgicalOptions.name: Gastric electrical stimulation (Enterra therapy)
surgicalOptions.description: A surgically or laparoscopically implanted neurostimulator delivers high-frequency, low-energy pulses to the gastric antrum via two intramuscular electrodes. It suppresses nausea and vomiting through central neural pathways rather than by improving emptying.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 50-60% of patients achieve a clinically meaningful reduction in vomiting episodes; device removal is required in around 5-10% due to infection or lead migration.

surgicalOptions.name: Per-oral pyloromyotomy (G-POEM)
surgicalOptions.description: An endoscopic procedure in which a submucosal tunnel is created and the circular muscle of the pylorus is divided, reducing pylorospasm and pyloric resistance without open surgery. Performed under general anaesthesia and typically requires one overnight stay.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Early and intermediate data show symptomatic improvement in 60-80% of patients and objective improvement in gastric emptying scintigraphy in 50-70%; long-term durability data are accumulating.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical pyloroplasty or pyloromyotomy
surgicalOptions.description: An open or laparoscopic widening of the pyloric outlet performed when endoscopic approaches are unavailable or have failed. Occasionally combined with gastric electrical stimulator placement in a single operation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Retrospective series report symptomatic response in 50-70% of carefully selected patients; considered when multiple medical therapies have failed and nutritional status is compromised.

alternativeTreatments.name: Small, frequent low-fat, low-fibre meals
alternativeTreatments.description: The single most evidence-based non-pharmacological intervention: fat and insoluble fibre are the two main dietary factors that delay gastric emptying. Eating five to six small meals daily and choosing soft or liquid-dominant foods reduces symptom burden in the majority of patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture and electroacupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Small randomised trials, predominantly from China, suggest that acupoints ST36 and PC6 may improve gastric emptying and nausea scores; evidence quality is moderate and most researchers consider it an adjunct rather than primary therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ginger supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Ginger has prokinetic properties in animal models and small human studies; 1-1.2 g daily may modestly reduce nausea though high-quality randomised data are lacking for gastroparesis specifically.

whySeeSpecialist: A gastroenterologist is needed whenever the diagnosis is uncertain or symptoms do not improve after initial dietary adjustment, because distinguishing gastroparesis from functional dyspepsia and from mechanical obstruction requires specialised testing unavailable in primary care. Specialist involvement is also essential for managing diabetic gastroparesis in coordination with an endocrinologist, for overseeing trials of prokinetic drugs with narrow safety margins, and for considering interventional procedures including G-POEM or gastric electrical stimulation in refractory cases.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a gastroenterologist with training in neurogastroenterology and motility disorders, ideally affiliated with a centre that performs four-hour scintigraphy and wireless motility capsule testing in-house. For diabetic gastroparesis, a combined clinic involving a gastroenterologist and an endocrinologist achieves better glucose management alongside motility treatment. Ask whether the centre offers G-POEM or has access to a surgeon performing surgical pyloromyotomy, as these options matter for refractory cases.
preventionStrategies: Maintain blood glucose as close to target as possible in diabetes to limit vagal nerve damage; each year of poor control adds cumulative risk.
Avoid opioids for pain management whenever alternative analgesics will suffice, particularly in patients with existing GI motility concerns.
Screen for and promptly treat hypothyroidism and other secondary causes when they are detected.
After upper GI or pancreatic surgery, begin early post-operative nutritional support and monitoring for delayed gastric emptying, which allows timely prokinetic treatment.
Discuss the gastroparesis risk associated with GLP-1 receptor agonists with your prescribing clinician, particularly if you already have nausea, early satiety, or prior GI motility symptoms.
lifestyleModifications: Eat five to six small meals rather than two or three large ones; the stomach empties smaller volumes far more reliably.
Choose soft, moist, or liquefied foods when solids are poorly tolerated; blending meals preserves nutrition while reducing particle size.
Limit fat to less than 40 g per meal, as fat is the strongest dietary brake on gastric emptying.
Reduce insoluble fibre from raw vegetables and whole grains, which can form bezoars in a poorly emptying stomach.
Walk for 15-20 minutes after eating; mild upright physical activity modestly accelerates gastric emptying.
Avoid lying down for at least two hours after a meal to allow gravity to assist emptying.
Limit carbonated drinks, alcohol, and tobacco, all of which impair gastric motility.
Keep a detailed food and symptom diary to identify personal trigger foods and the meal sizes that cause flares.

recommended: Low-fat (less than 40 g per meal) soft or liquid-dominant foods
Small portions of easily digestible protein such as eggs, fish, and low-fat poultry
Well-cooked vegetables without skins; strained vegetable soups
Nutritional oral supplements (e.g. Ensure, Boost) when solid intake is too low to meet energy needs
Adequate hydration with small frequent sips rather than large volumes at once
avoid: High-fat meals including fried foods, full-fat dairy, fatty meats, and butter-heavy dishes
Raw vegetables, whole grains, beans, and other high-insoluble-fibre foods that can clump into bezoars
Carbonated beverages, which increase gastric distension and worsen bloating
Alcohol, which delays gastric emptying and increases nausea
Very large portions; eating until completely full is a reliable trigger for prolonged nausea

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate exercise, particularly walking after meals, provides modest benefit for gastric emptying and is encouraged. Intense aerobic exercise — running, cycling at high effort — can paradoxically reduce splanchnic blood flow and worsen nausea in some patients, so intensity should be titrated to tolerance. In patients with severe weight loss or active vomiting, rest and nutritional recovery take priority over structured exercise until stability is achieved.
dailyManagement: Follow a consistent eating schedule of five to six small meals at regular intervals rather than eating ad hoc.
Monitor blood glucose before and two hours after each meal in diabetic gastroparesis; aim to keep readings below 10 mmol/L to avoid acute motility impairment.
Take prokinetic medications 15-30 minutes before each meal as directed for maximum effect.
Keep a food and symptom log to identify patterns and report them to your gastroenterologist at follow-up.
Have an emergency plan — including who to call and what antiemetics are available — for flares that prevent oral intake for more than 24 hours.
Review all medications with your gastroenterologist annually, as some agents newly added for other conditions can worsen gastroparesis silently.
prognosis: The outlook for gastroparesis depends heavily on its underlying cause. Idiopathic post-infectious gastroparesis has the best prognosis: studies suggest that approximately 70-80% of younger patients experience significant improvement or resolution of symptoms within one to two years, and some achieve normal emptying times. Diabetic gastroparesis is more variable; symptoms wax and wane with glycaemic control, and patients who achieve sustained HbA1c improvement can see meaningful motility recovery, though a proportion progress despite optimised treatment. Post-surgical gastroparesis resolves within weeks to months in most cases when the vagal injury is incomplete. Overall, modern management allows the majority of patients to maintain adequate oral nutrition without tube feeding. However, approximately 5-10% of patients with severe refractory disease require long-term nutritional support, and quality of life can remain substantially impaired in this group. Hospitalisation rates are high — one U.S. study found an average of 1.4 admissions per patient per year — underscoring the importance of an established specialist relationship.
recoveryTimeline: Post-infectious idiopathic gastroparesis often shows progressive symptom improvement over three to twelve months, with the majority of young, otherwise healthy patients substantially better within two years. Dietary modification and prokinetics reduce symptoms within days to weeks in most patients, though objective improvement in emptying times may lag. Diabetic gastroparesis does not have a defined recovery arc; it fluctuates chronically but responds to glycaemic optimisation over months. After G-POEM, most patients notice symptomatic benefit within four to twelve weeks. After gastric stimulator implantation, the full response is usually apparent by three months.
complications: Severe malnutrition and cachexia from chronically inadequate caloric intake, sometimes requiring nasojejunal or jejunostomy tube feeding.
Gastric bezoar — a concretion of undigested food and fibre that can cause mechanical gastric outlet obstruction, requiring endoscopic fragmentation.
Aspiration pneumonia from vomiting of retained gastric contents, particularly during sleep or in patients with impaired protective reflexes.
Severe and unpredictable glycaemic fluctuations in diabetic patients, including recurrent hypoglycaemia from insulin given in anticipation of a meal that empties hours later.
Oesophagitis and Barrett oesophagus from chronic acid reflux driven by a chronically full stomach.
Depression and anxiety, which affect approximately 40% of patients with chronic gastroparesis and independently worsen symptom severity and quality of life.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Gastroparesis
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient overview of gastroparesis symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

supportResources.name: American Neurogastroenterology and Motility Society
supportResources.description: Patient resources from the specialty society for GI motility disorders, including gastroparesis diet guides and provider directories.

supportResources.name: Gastroparesis Patient Association for Cures and Treatments (G-PACT)
supportResources.description: Non-profit advocacy and support organisation for gastroparesis patients providing educational materials and peer support networks.

sources.title: Camilleri M et al. Clinical Guideline: Management of Gastroparesis. American Journal of Gastroenterology, 2013
Parkman HP et al. Dietary Intake and Nutritional Deficiencies in Patients with Diabetic or Idiopathic Gastroparesis. Gastroenterology, 2011
Choung RS et al. Prevalence and Sociodemographic Factors of Functional Dyspepsia and Gastroparesis — A Population-Based Study. Gut, 2012
Malik Z et al. Gastric Electrical Stimulation in Refractory Gastroparesis: A Review of Clinical Studies. Gastroenterology Clinics of North America, 2015
Khashab MA et al. International Consensus Recommendations on the Classification, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Refractory Gastroparesis. Annals of Surgery, 2020
Parkman HP. Gastroparesis: Symptoms, Evaluation, and Treatment. Gastroenterology Clinics of North America, 2015

confusedWithConditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Functional dyspepsia produces identical upper GI symptoms but gastric emptying scintigraphy is normal; the two can coexist and are distinguished only by formal testing.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gastroesophageal reflux disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: GERD causes heartburn and regurgitation from acid reflux, not from a stomach that fails to empty; gastroparesis causes secondary reflux, so both conditions can be present simultaneously.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peptic ulcer disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peptic ulcers cause nausea and early satiety through pain and pyloric oedema; upper endoscopy quickly distinguishes an ulcer from impaired motility with a normal mucosal appearance.

confusedWithConditions.name: Achlorhydria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Achlorhydria is absent gastric acid production causing nausea and bloating but with normal or accelerated rather than delayed gastric emptying; diagnosed by gastric pH testing or gastrin levels.

relatedConditions.name: Achlorhydria
relatedConditions.relevance: Both reflect impaired gastric physiology; achlorhydria often accompanies autonomic neuropathy and can compound the nutritional consequences of gastroparesis.

relatedConditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
relatedConditions.relevance: Functional dyspepsia and gastroparesis overlap symptomatically and by mechanism; a subset of functional dyspepsia patients have mildly delayed emptying on scintigraphy.

relatedConditions.name: Gastroesophageal reflux disease
relatedConditions.relevance: A full, poorly emptying stomach increases reflux pressure, making GERD a near-universal co-morbidity in gastroparesis patients.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: Diabetes is the most common identifiable cause of gastroparesis and the primary target for secondary prevention.

faqs.question: What is gastroparesis?
faqs.answer: Gastroparesis is a condition in which the stomach empties much more slowly than normal because its muscles do not contract strongly enough. Food sits in the stomach for hours longer than it should, causing nausea, vomiting, bloating, and early fullness. It is diagnosed with a stomach-emptying scan and is most common in people with diabetes.

faqs.question: What causes gastroparesis?
faqs.answer: The three most common causes are long-standing diabetes (which damages the nerve controlling the stomach), a prior viral illness (which can injure the stomach's pacemaker cells), and surgery near the stomach or pancreas. Opioid pain medications and some other drugs can also slow emptying. In about a third of cases no clear cause is found.

faqs.question: How is gastroparesis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The standard test is a four-hour gastric emptying scan: you eat a small radiolabelled egg-white meal and a camera measures how much remains in your stomach at one, two, and four hours. More than 10% remaining at four hours confirms delayed emptying. An endoscopy is usually done first to rule out a blockage or ulcer.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of gastroparesis?
faqs.answer: Nausea (present most days), vomiting of partially digested food hours after eating, feeling full after just a few bites, upper abdominal bloating and pain, and unintentional weight loss are the most common symptoms. In diabetic patients, unpredictable blood glucose swings are often the first clue that the stomach is not emptying normally.

faqs.question: Can gastroparesis be cured?
faqs.answer: Post-viral idiopathic gastroparesis often improves significantly on its own within one to two years, especially in younger patients. Diabetic and post-surgical gastroparesis can be managed but rarely cure completely. Treating the underlying cause — tightening blood sugar control, stopping an offending drug, managing thyroid disease — can produce substantial improvement.

faqs.question: What is the best diet for gastroparesis?
faqs.answer: Eat five to six small meals a day rather than two or three large ones. Choose soft, moist, or blended foods and limit fat to below 40 g per meal, as fat strongly delays stomach emptying. Avoid raw vegetables, skins, and seeds that can clump in the stomach. Nutritional shakes can supplement calories when solid intake is too low.

faqs.question: What medications treat gastroparesis?
faqs.answer: Metoclopramide is the only FDA-approved prokinetic; it speeds emptying and reduces nausea but is limited to 12-week courses to avoid a movement-disorder side effect. Domperidone (not FDA-approved but available in some countries), erythromycin at low doses, and antiemetics such as ondansetron help control symptoms. All should be supervised by a gastroenterologist.

faqs.question: How does diabetes cause gastroparesis?
faqs.answer: Years of high blood sugar damage the vagus nerve — the main wire controlling stomach contractions — and destroy the stomach's pacemaker cells. High blood glucose also directly impairs contraction strength on a short-term basis, so even a single high reading can trigger a flare. Better glucose control slows the nerve damage and can partially restore emptying.

faqs.question: Is gastroparesis life-threatening?
faqs.answer: Gastroparesis itself is not usually directly life-threatening, but severe cases cause malnutrition, dehydration, and dangerous blood-glucose swings that can be serious. Aspiration pneumonia from vomiting during sleep is a real risk. Most patients manage well with dietary changes and medication, but about 5-10% with severe disease need tube feeding to stay nourished.

faqs.question: Can gastroparesis cause weight loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. Persistent nausea and early fullness reduce how much a person can eat, and when caloric intake falls below energy needs, weight loss follows. Losing more than 5 kg warrants a nutritional assessment by a dietitian familiar with gastroparesis, and some patients require oral nutritional supplements or tube feeding to stop further deterioration.

faqs.question: What is a gastric emptying scan?
faqs.answer: A gastric emptying scan is the key test for gastroparesis. You eat a small standardised meal — typically scrambled egg whites — that has been labelled with a harmless radioactive tracer. A camera measures how much of the meal remains in your stomach at one, two, and four hours. Retaining more than 10% at four hours confirms gastroparesis. The test takes about four hours and involves no injections.

faqs.question: What is the difference between gastroparesis and functional dyspepsia?
faqs.answer: Both conditions cause nausea, bloating, and early fullness, but in functional dyspepsia the gastric emptying scan is normal. Gastroparesis is confirmed only when the scan shows retained food at four hours. The two conditions can overlap — a person with functional dyspepsia may have mildly slow emptying — but true gastroparesis requires the objective scintigraphy finding.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with gastroparesis?
faqs.answer: Light to moderate exercise, especially a 15-20 minute walk after meals, is beneficial and modestly speeds stomach emptying. Intense exercise can worsen nausea during a flare, so listen to your body. During periods of significant weight loss or vomiting, rest and nutrition take priority, and a gradual return to activity should be guided by how you feel.

faqs.question: What is G-POEM for gastroparesis?
faqs.answer: G-POEM (gastric per-oral endoscopic myotomy) is a procedure where an endoscopist cuts the pyloric muscle from inside through a small tunnel under the stomach lining, widening the exit of the stomach without external incisions. About 60-80% of carefully selected patients report symptom improvement, and most go home the next day. It is offered when diet and medications have not worked.

faqs.question: Can gastroparesis cause bezoars?
faqs.answer: Yes. When food fibres, skins, and seeds accumulate in a stomach that cannot empty them, they can compact into a solid mass called a bezoar. Bezoars cause a sensation of fullness, worsening nausea, and can partially block the stomach outlet. Endoscopic fragmentation removes most bezoars. Limiting fibrous foods and fruits with skin reduces the risk.

faqs.question: Does gastroparesis affect blood sugar in diabetics?
faqs.answer: Significantly. Because the stomach empties unpredictably, glucose from a meal enters the bloodstream at variable and often delayed times, making insulin timing difficult. This causes post-meal hypoglycaemia when insulin peaks before the food arrives, followed by delayed hyperglycaemia. Continuous glucose monitoring and insulin pump therapy can help manage this variability.

faqs.question: When should I go to the emergency room for gastroparesis?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care if you cannot keep any liquids down for more than 48 hours, if you have signs of dehydration (dry mouth, no urination, dizziness), or if blood glucose cannot be controlled at home. Severe worsening of abdominal pain beyond your usual pattern — which could indicate a bezoar blocking the stomach — also warrants emergency assessment.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from gastroparesis?
faqs.answer: Recovery depends on the cause. Post-viral idiopathic gastroparesis improves significantly in most patients within one to two years. Dietary changes and prokinetics usually reduce symptoms within days to weeks. After G-POEM, most patients notice benefit within four to twelve weeks. Diabetic gastroparesis does not follow a fixed recovery arc but improves when blood sugar is consistently well controlled.

faqs.question: Does stress worsen gastroparesis?
faqs.answer: Psychological stress directly alters gut motility through the brain-gut axis and can trigger or worsen gastroparesis flares. Anxiety and depression, which affect about 40% of gastroparesis patients, independently increase symptom severity. Cognitive-behavioural therapy, gut-directed hypnotherapy, and stress management techniques are valuable adjuncts to physical treatment.

faqs.question: Can children get gastroparesis?
faqs.answer: Yes, though it is less common than in adults. Paediatric gastroparesis most often follows a viral illness or results from a neurological condition. Children may present primarily with poor appetite, refusal to eat, and failure to grow rather than the classic adult symptoms of nausea and vomiting. Paediatric gastroenterology referral is important for accurate diagnosis and weight-appropriate management.

metaDescription: Gastroparesis slows stomach emptying, causing nausea, vomiting and early fullness. Learn causes, diagnosis and treatment options. Doctor-reviewed.
